skill.name: git-commit-helper
skill.description: Generate Conventional Commit messages from staged git changes and guide users through clear commit message structure.
skill.summary: Draft concise Conventional Commit messages from git diffs and staged change context.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and blocker patterns, but review found documentation examples rather than executable code. The weak cryptography alerts are false positives from ordinary words and commit-message text, and the network alert is a Claude Code attribution URL in an example. The remaining risk is low because the skill encourages git commands that can change repository history when used by a user.

low_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Git Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes git commands such as staging, committing, and amending commits. These are expected for a commit helper, but users should review commands before execution because they can modify repository state or history.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep commands explicit and require user confirmation before staging, committing, or amending changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples for git workflows. They are legitimate for this skill, but they can modify local repository state when executed.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Attribution URL Is Non-Executable
low_findings.description: The only network-related static hit is a Claude Code attribution link inside a sample commit message. No code performs a request or sends repository data to this URL.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required, but keep attribution examples clearly separate from executable instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as markdown content in an example commit footer. I found no executable network call in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static Cryptography and Shell Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The blocker alerts for weak cryptography and many shell execution alerts are caused by markdown examples, code fences, inline git commands, and ordinary words such as description. No Ruby backtick execution or cryptographic implementation is present.
low_findings.recommendation: Treat these scanner results as dismissed unless future versions add executable scripts or dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both scanned files found markdown documentation only. The flagged text does not execute commands, implement cryptography, or hide malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests git status, git diff, git add, git commit, and git commit --amend commands. These commands are appropriate, but command execution should remain user-controlled.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Do not auto-run modifying git commands without explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact commands are present in the documentation. Their purpose is legitimate, but they affect files or commit history if executed.

content.value_statement: Writing useful commit messages takes time and can be inconsistent across teams. This skill reviews staged git changes and helps produce concise Conventional Commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged git diffs to identify the main change type and scope.
Suggests Conventional Commit subjects, bodies, and breaking-change notes.
Explains when to use feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, chore, and related types.
Helps refine vague commit summaries into imperative, specific messages.
Provides examples for simple, scoped, multi-file, and breaking-change commits.
limitations: It depends on available git diff context and may miss unstaged changes.
It does not validate project-specific commit policies unless the user provides them.
It cannot guarantee that a commit message matches reviewer expectations.
It should not run modifying git commands without explicit user confirmation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Commit
use_cases.description: Turn staged feature work into a clear Conventional Commit with a useful scope and short body.

use_cases.title: Review Mixed Changes Before Commit
use_cases.description: Check staged files and decide whether the changes need one commit or several focused commits.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team History
use_cases.description: Guide contributors toward consistent commit types, summaries, and breaking-change notes.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Commit Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one staged change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message with a short subject.

prompt_templates.title: Add Scope and Body
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a more complete message
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the staged diff, choose the best type and scope, and write a commit subject plus a brief body explaining why.

prompt_templates.title: Split Mixed Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the staged changes and tell me whether they should be split into multiple commits. Suggest messages for each logical commit.

prompt_templates.title: Check Release Impact
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the staged changes for breaking behavior, then draft a Conventional Commit message with any required footer or migration note.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add password reset endpoints and email token handling.
output_examples.output:
feat(auth): add password reset flow

Adds reset request and token validation support so users can recover accounts securely.

output_examples.input: Staged changes fix a crash when profile data is missing.
output_examples.output:
fix(profile): handle missing user details

Adds null checks before reading optional profile fields.

output_examples.input: Staged changes update API response fields in a breaking way.
output_examples.output:
feat(api)!: rename response metadata fields

BREAKING CHANGE: clients must read pagination data from meta.page instead of paging.

best_practices: Review the staged diff before asking for a final commit message.
Keep each commit focused on one logical change whenever possible.
Include breaking-change notes and issue references when they apply.
anti_patterns: Using vague subjects such as update, changes, or fix stuff.
Combining unrelated feature, refactor, and formatting changes in one commit.
Amending or committing changes without reviewing the staged content first.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commit message guidance and command examples. Users should approve any git command before execution.

faq.question: Which commit format does it use?
faq.answer: It follows Conventional Commits, including type, optional scope, summary, body, and footer guidance.

faq.question: Can it handle breaking changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest the exclamation marker or BREAKING CHANGE footer when the diff indicates incompatible behavior.

faq.question: Does it read unstaged files?
faq.answer: The workflow focuses on staged changes. Ask it to review unstaged changes separately if needed.

faq.question: Can teams customize the style?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the team commit rules, scopes, or examples, and the assistant can follow them.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. The reviewed files are documentation, and the git commands are visible examples for user-controlled workflows.
